title: Bottling and labelling honey
subtitle: Getting the harvest into jars cleanly.
excerpt: Raw honey extracted from the super needs time to settle before it goes into jars. Rush the bottling and you get foam-topped jars and air pockets. Allow the right settling window, skim once, bottle at the correct temperature, and the result is clear, correctly labelled honey that keeps indefinitely.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com, post-extraction processing and storage guidance. BBKA guide to honey labelling requirements for UK beekeepers (2023). Food Standards Agency, UK food labelling guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measure of dissolved solids in a liquid, used in honey to assess water content. Honey below 20% water content (around 80 Brix) resists fermentation. A refractometer reads Brix directly.

The natural crystallisation of honey as glucose precipitates out of solution. All honey granulates eventually; the rate depends on the glucose-to-fructose ratio and storage temperature. Granulated honey is not spoiled.

content.text: Fresh honey from the extractor contains wax fragments, air bubbles, and bits of comb. Left in a

-free settling tank for two to three days, the debris rises to the surface and the honey below it becomes clear. Bottle too soon and the jars look messy; bottle too late and the honey starts to set in the tank.

Pour the extracted honey through a coarse strainer into the settling tank and cover the top. Leave at room temperature (20 to 25°C is ideal) for two days minimum, three for slow-settling honeys such as oilseed rape. Skim the foam and wax crust off the surface with a flat spoon before opening the gate valve. Do not stir.
Before bottling, check the water content with a refractometer. Place a drop of honey on the prism and read the

scale. Honey above 20% water will ferment in the jar. If the reading is marginal, the honey was extracted from uncapped or under-ripe frames; store it separately and use it for cooking rather than direct sale.

Wash glass jars and lids in hot soapy water, rinse well, then place upside down on a clean baking tray and heat in the oven at 100°C for 15 minutes. Let them cool slightly before filling, a sudden temperature change can crack glass. Plastic squeeze jars are a valid alternative; wash with boiling water and allow to dry completely.

Open the settling tank gate valve slowly. Fill each jar to within 10 mm of the rim. Close and tighten the lid immediately. Invert the jar for 30 seconds to sterilise the inside of the lid with the warm honey, then stand it upright to cool. Label once fully cool. A jar filled at 40°C and labelled immediately will condensate if moved to a cool room.

attrs.heading: Minimum label information for UK honey

content.text: A honey label sold at the farm gate or market must show: the word HONEY; the net weight in grams; a best-before date; and the producer's name and address. Country of origin is required unless you sell directly to the customer at your own premises.

Store labelled jars in a dark, cool, dry cupboard. Honey absorbs moisture from the air; a loosely sealed jar in a humid kitchen will dilute and eventually ferment. Honey kept correctly does not spoil, but
at 14°C is rapid. If selling, most customers prefer runny honey; warm the jar gently in a basin of water at 40°C to re-liquify. Above 45°C and HMF levels rise, which degrades quality.
